title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a student called Priya talking about a college radio project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a student called Priya talking about a college radio project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A student called Priya talks about working on a college radio station.
extracts.audio_script: Hi. I’m Priya, and I help run Campus Radio, our college station. A lot of people assume we broadcast from a room in the students’ union because events happen there, but our studios are in the new [9]media centre[/9], beside the main library. The station started online four years ago and can now be heard around the college throughout the day. Students from all subjects can join; you do not need to be studying journalism or music. I joined because I was nervous about public speaking and wanted to become more confident.||Programmes include music, sports news, interviews and information about events. When I began, I expected to choose songs, but my earliest job was reading short [10]weather reports[/10] at the end of the breakfast programme. It seemed a small task, yet it taught me how to speak clearly and keep to a fixed time. New members attend two training sessions before being allowed on air. You do not need your own microphone or laptop, though we ask everyone to bring personal [11]headphones[/11], as they are more comfortable and hygienic than sharing.||Recording a programme is only part of the process. We often remove mistakes, adjust volume or add short pieces of music afterwards. New members therefore learn basic [12]editing software[/12] during their second training session. We used to keep hundreds of CDs in the studio, and some still remain for decoration. The tracks used on current programmes are stored in a digital [13]music library[/13], where presenters can search by performer or style. Our live weekly programme was once broadcast on Friday afternoons, but students were often travelling home. It now goes out on [14]Thursday lunchtime[/14], when the café and common rooms are full of listeners.||Campus Radio can help students develop many abilities. A professional presenter recently visited to talk about voice control, but members told us they needed most support with speaking to guests. Therefore, our next specialist session will focus on [15]interview skills[/15], including how to prepare questions and listen carefully to answers. We are also expanding beyond live broadcasting. This term, members are creating a [16]podcast series[/16] about unusual student hobbies, so listeners can download episodes whenever they like. If you have an interesting activity, we may ask you to be one of our guests.||The station supports a different organisation each year. Last year listeners donated books for a primary school; this term, our fundraising will assist the [17]local hospital[/17], which is developing a room for young patients to relax in. We will advertise events and collect donations during broadcasts. Anyone interested in joining can visit us before making a decision. Instead of asking people to book individual visits, we are holding an [18]open day[/18] next Wednesday, with studio tours and the chance to record a short announcement. Details are on the college website. I hope to see some of you there.

dialogue.speaker: priya
dialogue.text: Hi. I’m Priya, and I help run Campus Radio, our college station. A lot of people assume we broadcast from a room in the students’ union because events happen there, but our studios are in the new media centre, beside the main library. The station started online four years ago and can now be heard around the college throughout the day. Students from all subjects can join; you do not need to be studying journalism or music. I joined because I was nervous about public speaking and wanted to become more confident.||Programmes include music, sports news, interviews and information about events. When I began, I expected to choose songs, but my earliest job was reading short weather reports at the end of the breakfast programme. It seemed a small task, yet it taught me how to speak clearly and keep to a fixed time. New members attend two training sessions before being allowed on air. You do not need your own microphone or laptop, though we ask everyone to bring personal headphones, as they are more comfortable and hygienic than sharing.||Recording a programme is only part of the process. We often remove mistakes, adjust volume or add short pieces of music afterwards. New members therefore learn basic editing software during their second training session. We used to keep hundreds of CDs in the studio, and some still remain for decoration. The tracks used on current programmes are stored in a digital music library, where presenters can search by performer or style. Our live weekly programme was once broadcast on Friday afternoons, but students were often travelling home. It now goes out on Thursday lunchtime, when the café and common rooms are full of listeners.||Campus Radio can help students develop many abilities. A professional presenter recently visited to talk about voice control, but members told us they needed most support with speaking to guests. Therefore, our next specialist session will focus on interview skills, including how to prepare questions and listen carefully to answers. We are also expanding beyond live broadcasting. This term, members are creating a podcast series about unusual student hobbies, so listeners can download episodes whenever they like. If you have an interesting activity, we may ask you to be one of our guests.||The station supports a different organisation each year. Last year listeners donated books for a primary school; this term, our fundraising will assist the local hospital, which is developing a room for young patients to relax in. We will advertise events and collect donations during broadcasts. Anyone interested in joining can visit us before making a decision. Instead of asking people to book individual visits, we are holding an open day next Wednesday, with studio tours and the chance to record a short announcement. Details are on the college website. I hope to see some of you there.
